title: Changes in ideology
description: Test your skills in this quiz on influences on citizens' ideology.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Chart depicting the % of registered voters who identify as or lean toward a party. In the total population, 50% identify as or lean Democratic, and 42% identify as or lean Republican. Millennials are 59% Democrat and 32% Republican. Gen Xers are 48% Democrat and 43% Republican. Boomers are 48% Democrat and 46% Republican. Silents are 43% Democrat and 52% Republican. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd9bd4b31a86fe5f7d5150dfdec38f77.png)

*Chart depicting party identification based on generation. Pew identifies millennials as individuals born between 1981 and 1996, Gen Xers as individuals born between 1965 and 1980, boomers as individuals born between 1946 to 1964, and silents as individuals born between 1928 and 1945. Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.people-press.org/2018/03/01/1-generations-party-identification-midterm-voting-preferences-views-of-trump/)*

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Party identification differs significantly between generations
Older generations tend to be more liberal than younger generations
Millennials are more likely to identify as Republicans than as Democrats
Gen Xers identify/lean more Democratic than the average survey respondent who expressed a preference for one of the two parties.

assessment_items.question_content:
![Chart depicting the % of registered voters who identify as or lean toward a party. In the total population, 50% identify as or lean Democratic, and 42% identify as or lean Republican. Millennials are 59% Democrat and 32% Republican. Gen Xers are 48% Democrat and 43% Republican. Boomers are 48% Democrat and 46% Republican. Silents are 43% Democrat and 52% Republican. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd9bd4b31a86fe5f7d5150dfdec38f77.png)

*Chart depicting party identification based on generation. Pew identifies millennials as individuals born between 1981 and 1996, Gen Xers as individuals born between 1965 and 1980, boomers as individuals born between 1946 to 1964, and silents as individuals born between 1928 and 1945. Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.people-press.org/2018/03/01/1-generations-party-identification-midterm-voting-preferences-views-of-trump/)*

**Which of the following is an accurate conclusion based on the chart and your knowledge of the impact of lifecycle effects on political attitudes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Individuals are likely to become more conservative as they age
Individuals are likely to become more liberal as they age
Individuals form political opinions as young adults and do not change them over time
Individuals are likely to become more politically active as they age

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following life events would be most likely to have a significant effect on an individual’s political attitude?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Getting married or divorced
Moving to a different state
Changing jobs
Graduating from high school

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d4969645146cecf140c0234f74d8cae6.png)

**Which of the following accurately describes the information in the bar graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Boomer and the Silent generations are more likely to support maintaining social security benefits than Millennials and Gen-Xers
Millennials and Gen-Xers are more likely to support avoiding cuts to Social Security than  the Boomer and the Silent generations
The Boomer and Silent generations are less likely to support maintaining benefits than the Millennials and Gen-Xers.
Millennials and Gen-Xers are less likely to support cutting social security benefits to reduce the deficit than the Boomer and Silent generations

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Percent who identify with a political ideology** ||
 | 18-29 | 30-44 | 45-64 | 65+ 
- | - | - | -| -
**Liberal** | $31\%$ | $23\%$ | $21\%$ | $16\%$ 
**Moderate** | $39\%$ | $41\%$ | $40\%$ | $39\%$
**Conservative** | $29\%$ | $36\%$ | $39\%$ | $45\%$ 

>Source: 2000 National Annenberg Election Study

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the chart? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People who are 65 years old or older are more likely to identify as conservative than liberal
People who are between the ages of 45 and 64 are more likely to be liberal than conservative
People who are 29 years old or younger are more likely to identify as conservative than liberal
People who are between the ages of 35 and 44 are more likely identify as liberal than moderate

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Percent who identify with a political ideology** ||
 | 18-29 | 30-44 | 45-64 | 65+ 
- | - | - | -| -
**Liberal** | $31\%$ | $23\%$ | $21\%$ | $16\%$ 
**Moderate** | $39\%$ | $41\%$ | $40\%$ | $39\%$
**Conservative** | $29\%$ | $36\%$ | $39\%$ | $45\%$ 

>Source: 2000 National Annenberg Election Study

**Based on the data shown in the table and your knowledge of political socialization, which of the following is the most likely implication of lifecycle effects on an individual’s political attitudes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As people age, they are more likely to identify as conservative
As people age, they are more likely to identify as liberal
As people age, they are more likely to identify as moderate
As people age, they show no major shifts in ideology

assessment_items.question_content:
![Line graph depicting the percent of Americans who said that immigrants strengthen the country from 1994 to 2017 organized by generation. The Millennial Generation was first tracked in 2003 and rose at incremental levels from 56 percent to 79 percent in 2017. Gen Xers began at 36% in 1994 and wavered over time, ending at 66% in 2017. The Boomer generation began at 31% in 1994, rose, fell again from 2002 to 2010, and then rose to 56%. The Silent Generation began at 29% in 1994, fluctuated a great deal, and ended at 47% in 2017. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64087a1352e1264e89fb73ccd4c1a7e1.png)

*Graph depicting the percentage of each generation who say that “immigrants today strengthen our country.”  Pew identifies millennials as individuals born between 1981 and 1996, Gen Xers as individuals born between 1965 and 1980, boomers as those born between 1946 to 1964, and silents as those born between 1928 and 1945. Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.people-press.org/2018/03/01/4-race-immigration-same-sex-marriage-abortion-global-warming-gun-policy-marijuana-legalization/)* 

**Based on the data shown in the graph, which of the following statements is true about generational opinions towards immigrants?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: In all generations, opinions towards immigrants have become more favorable over time
Gen Xers have always had more favorable opinions towards immigrants than boomers
The silent generation had the largest increase in favorable opinions toward immigrants over the period shown
Across all generations, opinions toward immigrants have become more favorable at a steady rate over time

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Winston Churchill is said to have remarked that ‘Any man who is not a socialist at the age of 21 has no heart, and any man who is still a socialist at the age of 40 has no head.’ The first part of the quote can be taken as saying that it is easy to favor economic equality when one is a young adult in the process of getting educated, finding a job, and beginning a career. But the second part implies that the notion of equality of income for all looks far different once one is middle aged and established in an occupation.”

> -Source: Martin P. Wattenberg, *Is Voting for Young People?* 2012

**Which of the following statements is most consistent with the author’s argument in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People’s political ideology changes as they get older
People’s political ideology remains the same as they age
People’s political ideology only changes after major political events
People’s political ideology is affected by generational differences, not lifecycle effects

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Support for Curbing Power Plant Emissions** ||
Age range | Favor | Oppose | Don’t know
- | - | - | -
All US Adults | 64 | 31 | 5
18-29 | 72 | 25 | 4
30-49 | 66 | 29 | 4
50-64 | 60 | 37 | 3
65 and older | 55 | 35 | 10

>-Source: Pew Research Center, [link to data](https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2015/07/01/americans-politics-and-science-issues/pi_2015-07-01_science-and-politics_2-11/)

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Generational divides can influence political attitudes towards major issues.
Older generations are more likely to support Democratic policies than younger generations.
As presidents introduced climate change legislation to their agenda, trust in government rose.
As climate change became more of a hot-button issue for Americans, people stopped having opinions on what the government should do.

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Support for Curbing Power Plant Emissions** ||
Age range | Favor | Oppose | Don’t know
- | - | - | -
All US Adults | 64 | 31 | 5
18-29 | 72 | 25 | 4
30-49 | 66 | 29 | 4
50-64 | 60 | 37 | 3
65 and older | 55 | 35 | 10

>-Source: Pew Research Center, [link to data](https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2015/07/01/americans-politics-and-science-issues/pi_2015-07-01_science-and-politics_2-11/)

**Which of the following is an accurate statement about the information in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Younger Americans are more likely to support green legislation.
Older Americans are more likely than younger Americans to support legislation that stops the effects of climate change.
Public support for climate change legislation has risen over the past twenty years.
Age has no effect on whether an individual supports green legislation.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The line graph is titled "Percent of adults who favor same-sex marriage, by generation (2001-2019)." There are four lines on the graph representing four generations: the Silent Generation, the Baby Boomers, Generation X, and Millennials. The X-axis indicates years from 2001 to 2019. The Y-axis indicates percentages from zero percent to eighty percent. All four lines increase from 2001 to 2019. The Silent Generation line starts at 21 percent in 2001 and ends at 45 percent in 2019. The Baby Boomers line starts at 32 percent in 2001 and ends at 51 percent in 2019. The Generation X line starts at 49 percent in 2001 and ends at 58 percent in 2019. The Millennials line starts at 51 percent in 2003 and ends at 74 percent in 2019.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64c1b6f48c52afff6b4e0ab06c68c5f2.svg)

>-Source: [Pew Research Center](https://www.pewforum.org/fact-sheet/changing-attitudes-on-gay-marriage/)

**The data displayed in the table above best supports which of the following statements?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The generation a person was born into can affect their attitudes toward social issues.
Political attitudes shift towards conservative as a person ages.
Political attitudes remain relatively the same as a person ages.
Lifecycle effects influence political attitudes more than generational effects.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The line graph is titled "Percent of adults who favor same-sex marriage, by generation (2001-2019)." There are four lines on the graph representing four generations: the Silent Generation, the Baby Boomers, Generation X, and Millennials. The X-axis indicates years from 2001 to 2019. The Y-axis indicates percentages from zero percent to eighty percent. All four lines increase from 2001 to 2019. The Silent Generation line starts at 21 percent in 2001 and ends at 45 percent in 2019. The Baby Boomers line starts at 32 percent in 2001 and ends at 51 percent in 2019. The Generation X line starts at 49 percent in 2001 and ends at 58 percent in 2019. The Millennials line starts at 51 percent in 2003 and ends at 74 percent in 2019.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64c1b6f48c52afff6b4e0ab06c68c5f2.svg)

>-Source: [Pew Research Center](https://www.pewforum.org/fact-sheet/changing-attitudes-on-gay-marriage/)

**Which of the following best describes a trend in the line graph above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Generation X and Millennials are more likely to support same-sex marriage than the Silent Generation and Baby Boomers.
The Silent Generation and Baby Boomers have become more opposed to same-sex marriage as Generation and Millennials have become more supportive.
All generations have become more opposed to same-sex marriage over the last twenty years as it has become a divisive issue.
There are no generational differences on public support for same-sex marriage in the United States.
